---
title: "PJM Methodology"
---

## Pipeline design

The analytical pipeline is:

1. `gridstatus` download layer for PJM
2. PJM-specific cleaning
3. Analytical harmonization into the working nodal schema
4. Feature engineering
5. Congestion metrics
6. Map and report export

## Core data fields

The harmonized schema centers on:

- `timestamp`
- `node_id`
- `node_name`
- `lmp`
- `energy`
- `congestion`
- `loss`
- optional `renewable_mw`, `load_mw`, `zone`, `latitude`, `longitude`

## Congestion metrics

::: {.metric-table}
| Metric | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `abs_congestion` | Direct congestion intensity |
| `price_separation` | Market-wide node divergence in a given interval |
| `hub_lmp` | Reference price used for node-to-hub comparisons |
| `node_to_hub_spread` | Local divergence from a configured market reference |
| `persistent_congestion_score` | Composite score for map prominence and screening |
| `negative_price_flag` | Abnormal pricing indicator |
| `renewable_penetration` | Renewable share proxy when load is available |
| `net_load_mw` | Load minus renewable generation |
:::

## Hub reference logic

`node_to_hub_spread` is now **configuration-driven**.

The pipeline first checks [config/markets.yml](config/markets.yml) for explicit PJM hub-reference rules. If a configured match is available, it is used as the reference LMP. If not, the code falls back to a heuristic hub detection rule and then, if needed, to market mean.

This hierarchy keeps the site usable with sample data while making the research definition of “hub” explicit and auditable.

## Publication note

This site is built with Quarto, but the interactive map HTML is generated by the Python pipeline. Quarto then copies those map artifacts into the published website.
